## Contents

# Transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article **relies excessively on [references](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability") to [primary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research")**. Please improve this article by adding [secondary or tertiary sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Primary,_secondary_and_tertiary_sources "Wikipedia:No original research").  *Find sources:* ["Transition metal dichalcogenide monolayers"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22Transition+metal+dichalcogenide+monolayers%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2014)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/9/99/Question_book-new.svg/60px-Question_book-new.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/Monolayer_TMDC_structure.jpg/500px-Monolayer_TMDC_structure.jpg)

**Transition-metal dichalcogenide** (**TMD** or **TMDC**) **monolayers** are atomically thin [semiconductors](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") of the type MX2, with M a [transition-metal](/wiki/Transition_metal "Transition metal") atom ([Mo](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum"), [W](/wiki/Tungsten "Tungsten"), etc.) and X a [chalcogen](/wiki/Chalcogen "Chalcogen") atom ([S](/wiki/Sulphur "Sulphur"), [Se](/wiki/Selenium "Selenium"), or [Te](/wiki/Tellurium "Tellurium")). One layer of M atoms is sandwiched between two layers of X atoms. They are part of the large family of so-called [2D materials](/wiki/Single-layer_materials "Single-layer materials"), named so to emphasize their extraordinary thinness. For example, a MoS2 monolayer is only 6.5 Å thick. The key feature of these materials is the interaction of large atoms in the 2D structure as compared with first-row [transition-metal dichalcogenides](/wiki/Transition-metal_dichalcogenide "Transition-metal dichalcogenide"), e.g., [WTe2](/wiki/Tungsten_ditelluride "Tungsten ditelluride") exhibits anomalous [giant magnetoresistance](/wiki/Giant_magnetoresistance "Giant magnetoresistance") and [superconductivity](/wiki/Superconductivity "Superconductivity").[[1]](#cite_note-Eftekhari-1)

The discovery of [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") shows how new physical properties emerge when a bulk crystal of macroscopic dimensions is thinned down to one atomic layer. Like [graphite](/wiki/Graphite "Graphite"), TMD bulk crystals are formed of monolayers bound to each other by [van-der-Waals attraction](/wiki/Van_der_Waals_force "Van der Waals force"). TMD monolayers have properties that are distinctly different from those of the [semimetal](/wiki/Semimetal "Semimetal") graphene:

The work on TMD monolayers is an emerging research and development field since the discovery of the direct bandgap[[2]](#cite_note-Splendiani-2) and the potential applications in electronics [[14]](#cite_note-14)[[3]](#cite_note-FET-3) and valley physics.[[7]](#cite_note-ValleyCao-7)[[8]](#cite_note-MakValley-8)[[9]](#cite_note-CuiValley-9) TMDs are often combined with other [2D materials](/wiki/Single-layer_materials "Single-layer materials") like [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") and [hexagonal boron nitride](/wiki/Hexagonal_boron_nitride "Hexagonal boron nitride") to make [van der Waals heterostructures](/wiki/Van_der_Waals_heterostructures "Van der Waals heterostructures"). These heterostructures need to be optimized to be possibly used as building blocks for many different devices such as transistors, [solar cells](/wiki/Solar_cell "Solar cell"), [LEDs](/wiki/LED "LED"), [photodetectors](/wiki/Photodetector "Photodetector"), [fuel cells](/wiki/Fuel_cell "Fuel cell"), [photocatalytic](/wiki/Photocatalysis "Photocatalysis") and sensing devices. Some of these devices are already used in everyday life and can become smaller, cheaper and more efficient by using TMD monolayers.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Crystal structure

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Molybdenite-3D-balls.png/250px-Molybdenite-3D-balls.png)

Transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) are composed of three atomic planes and often two atomic species: a metal and two chalcogens. The honeycomb, hexagonal lattice has threefold symmetry and can permit mirror plane symmetry and/or inversion symmetry.[[17]](#cite_note-hovden2019-17) In the macroscopic bulk crystal, or more precisely, for an even number of monolayers, the crystal structure has an inversion center. In the case of a monolayer (or any odd number of layers), the crystal may or may not have an inversion center.

## Broken inversion symmetry

Two important consequences of that are:

These properties indicate that TMD monolayers represent a promising platform to explore spin and valley physics with the corresponding possible applications.

## Properties

### Transport properties

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/Field_Effect_Transistor_MoS2.jpg/500px-Field_Effect_Transistor_MoS2.jpg)

At submicron scales, 3D materials no longer have the same behavior as their 2D form, which can be an advantage. For example, [graphene](/wiki/Graphene "Graphene") has a very high [carrier mobility](/wiki/Electron_mobility "Electron mobility"), and accompanying lower losses through the [Joule effect](/wiki/Joule_effect "Joule effect"). But graphene has zero [bandgap](/wiki/Bandgap "Bandgap"), which results in a disqualifyingly low [on/off ratio](/wiki/Contrast_ratio "Contrast ratio") in transistor applications. TMD monolayers might be an alternative: they are structurally stable, display a band gap and show electron mobilities comparable to those of [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon"), so they can be used to fabricate transistors.

Although thin-layer TMDs have been found to have a lower electron mobility than bulk TMDs, most likely because their thinness makes them more susceptible to damage, it has been found that coating the TMDs with HfO2 or hexagonal boron nitride (hBN) increases their effective carrier mobility.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Optical properties

Theoretical energies of the A and B [excitons](/wiki/Exciton "Exciton")[[26]](#cite_note-26)

|  |  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | A (eV) | A (nm) | B (eV) | B (nm) |
| MoS2 | 1.78 | 695 | 1.96 | 632 |
| MoSe2 | 1.50 | 825 | 1.75 | 708 |
| MoTe2 | 1.06 | 1170 | 1.36 | 912 |
| WS2 | 1.84 | 673 | 2.28 | 544 |
| WSe2 | 1.52 | 815 | 2.00 | 620 |

A [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") can absorb [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon") with energy larger than or equal to its bandgap. This means that light with a shorter wavelength is absorbed. Semiconductors are typically efficient emitters if the minimum of the [conduction band](/wiki/Valence_and_conduction_bands "Valence and conduction bands") energy is at the same position in [k-space](/wiki/Momentum_space "Momentum space") as the maximum of the valence band, i.e., the band gap is direct. The band gap of bulk TMD material down to a thickness of two monolayers is still indirect, so the emission efficiency is lower compared to monolayered materials. The emission efficiency is about 104 greater for TMD monolayer than for bulk material.[[4]](#cite_note-SundaramLED-4) The band gaps of TMD monolayers are in the visible range (between 400 nm and 700 nm). The direct emission shows two [excitonic](/wiki/Exciton "Exciton") transitions called A and B, separated by the [spin–orbit coupling](/wiki/Spin%E2%80%93orbit_interaction "Spin–orbit interaction") energy. The lowest energy and therefore most important in intensity is the A emission.[[2]](#cite_note-Splendiani-2)[[27]](#cite_note-Berkeley-27) Owing to their direct band gap, TMD monolayers are promising materials for [optoelectronics](/wiki/Optoelectronics "Optoelectronics") applications.

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Fotodetector_de_MoS2.jpg/500px-Fotodetector_de_MoS2.jpg)

Atomic layers of MoS2 have been used as a [phototransistor](/wiki/Photodiode "Photodiode") and ultrasensitive detectors. Phototransistors are important devices: the first with a MoS2 monolayer active region shows a photoresponsivity of 7.5 mA W−1 which is similar to graphene devices that reach 6.1 mA W−1. Multilayer MoS2 show higher photoresponsivities, about 100 mA W−1, which is similar to silicon devices. Making a gold contact at the far edges of a monolayer allows an ultrasensitive detector to be fabricated.[[5]](#cite_note-photodetector-5) Such a detector has a photoresponsivity reaching 880 A W−1, 106 greater than the first graphene [photodetectors](/wiki/Photodetector "Photodetector"). This high degree of electrostatic control is due to the thin active region of the monolayer. Its simplicity and the fact that it has only one semiconductor region, whereas the current generation of photodetectors is typically a [p–n junction](/wiki/P%E2%80%93n_junction "P–n junction"), makes possible industrial applications such as high-sensitivity and flexible photodetectors. The only limitation for currently available devices is the slow photoresponse dynamics.[[5]](#cite_note-photodetector-5) Utilizing WSe2 the photoresponse was improved to a bandwidth of over 230 MHz by device symmetry optimization[[28]](#cite_note-28)

### Mechanical properties

Interest in the use of TMD monolayers such as MoS2, WS2, and WSe2 for the use in flexible electronics due to a change from an indirect band gap in 3D to a direct band gap in 2D emphasizes the importance of the mechanical properties of these materials.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Unlike in bulk samples it is much more difficult to uniformly deform 2D monolayers of material and as a result, taking mechanical measurements of 2D systems is more challenging. A method that was developed to overcome this challenge, called [atomic force microscopy](/wiki/Atomic_force_microscopy "Atomic force microscopy") (AFM) nanoindentation, involves bending a 2D monolayer suspended over a holey substrate with an AFM cantilever and measuring the applied force and displacement.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Through this method, defect free mechanically exfoliated monolayer flakes of MoS2 were found to have a [Young's modulus](/wiki/Young%27s_modulus "Young's modulus") of 270 GPa with a maximum experienced strain of 10% before breaking.[[31]](#cite_note-:2-31) In the same study, it was found that bilayer mechanically exfoliated MoS2 flakes have a lower Young's modulus of 200 GPa, which is attributed to interlayer sliding and defects in the monolayer.[[31]](#cite_note-:2-31) With increasing flake thickness the bending rigidity of the flake plays a dominant role and it is found that the Young's modulus of multilayer, 5- 25 layers, mechanically exfoliated MoS2 flakes is 330 GPa.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

The mechanical properties of other TMDs such as WS2 and WSe2 have also been determined. The Young's modulus of multilayer, 5-14 layers, mechanically exfoliated WSe2 is found to be 167 GPa with a maximum strain of 7%.[[33]](#cite_note-33) For WS2, the Young's modulus of chemical vapor deposited monolayer flakes is 272 GPa.[[34]](#cite_note-:3-34) From this same study the Young's modulus of CVD-grown monolayer flakes of MoS2 is found to be 264 GPa.[[34]](#cite_note-:3-34) This is an interesting result as the Young's modulus of the exfoliated MoS2 flake is nearly the same as that of the CVD grown MoS2 flake. It is generally accepted that chemically vapor deposited TMDs will include more defects when compared with the mechanically exfoliated films that are obtained from bulk single crystals, which implies that defects (points defects, etc.) that are included in the flake do not drastically affect the strength of the flake itself.

Under the application of strain, a decrease in the direct and indirect band gap is measured that is approximately linear with strain.[[35]](#cite_note-35) Importantly, the indirect bandgap decreases faster with applied strain to the monolayer than the direct bandgap, resulting in a crossover from direct to indirect band gap at a strain level of around 1%.[[36]](#cite_note-36) As a result, the emission efficiency of monolayers is expected to decrease for highly strained samples.[[37]](#cite_note-37) This property allows mechanical tuning of the electronic structure and also the possibility of fabrication of devices on flexible substrates.

## Fabrication of TMD monolayers

### Exfoliation

Exfoliation is a [top down](/wiki/Top-down_and_bottom-up_design "Top-down and bottom-up design") approach. In the bulk form, TMDs are crystals made of layers, which are coupled by Van-der-Waals forces. These interactions are weaker than the [chemical bonds](/wiki/Chemical_bond "Chemical bond") between the Mo and S in MoS2, for example. So TMD monolayers can be produced by micromechanical cleavage, just as graphene.

The crystal of TMD is rubbed against the surface of another material (any solid surface). In practice, adhesive tape is placed on the TMD bulk material and subsequently removed. The adhesive tape, with tiny TMD flakes coming off the bulk material, is brought down onto a substrate. On removing the adhesive tape from the substrate, TMD monolayer and multilayer flakes are deposited. This technique produces small samples of monolayer material, typically about 5–10 micrometers in diameter.[[38]](#cite_note-:4-38)

Large quantities of exfoliated material can also be produced using liquid-phase exfoliation by blending TMD materials with solvents and polymers.[[39]](#cite_note-liqExfo-39)

### Chemical vapor deposition

[Chemical vapor deposition](/wiki/Chemical_vapor_deposition "Chemical vapor deposition") (CVD) is another approach used to synthesize transition-metal dichalcogenides.[[40]](#cite_note-40) It has been used broadly to synthesize many different TMDs because it can be easily adapted for different TMD materials. Generally, CVD growth of TMDs is achieved by putting precursors to the material, typically a transition-metal oxide and pure chalcogen, into a furnace with the substrate on which the material will form.[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) The furnace is heated to high temperatures (anywhere from 650 to 1000 °C) with an inert gas, typically N2 or Ar, flowing through the tube.[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) Some materials require H2 gas as a catalyst for formation, so it may be flowed through the furnace in smaller quantities than the inert gas.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

Outside of traditional CVD, metal organic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) has been used to synthesize TMDs. Unlike traditional CVD described above, MOCVD uses gaseous precursors, as opposed to solid precursors and MOCVD is usually carried out at lower temperatures, anywhere from 300 to 900 °C.[[43]](#cite_note-43) MOCVD has been shown to provide more consistent wafer-scale growth than traditional CVD.

CVD is often used over mechanical exfoliation despite its added complexity because it can produce monolayers ranging anywhere from 5 to 100 microns in size as opposed to the surface areas of roughly 5-10 microns produced using the mechanical exfoliation method.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Not only do TMD monolayers produced by CVD have a larger surface area than those flakes produced by mechanical exfoliation, they are often more uniform. Monolayer TMD flakes with very little or no multilayer areas can be produced by chemical vapor deposition, in contrast to samples produced by mechanical exfoliation, which often have many multilayered areas.[[38]](#cite_note-:4-38)[[41]](#cite_note-:5-41) An alternative method has demonstrated that transition metal sulfides, including those of Ti, Zr, Hf, V, Nb, Ta, Cr, Mo, and W, can be synthesized through sulfurization of metal oxides in CS₂ vapor, achieving gram-scale production with simpler equipment and precursors.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Geometrically confined-growth techniques are also recently applied to realize wafer-scale single-domain TMD monolayer arrays and their heterostructures.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

### Molecular-beam epitaxy

[Molecular-beam epitaxy](/wiki/Molecular-beam_epitaxy "Molecular-beam epitaxy") (MBE) is an established technique for growing semiconductor devices with atomic monolayer thickness control. MBE has been used to grow different TMDs, such as MoSe2, WSe2, and early [transition metals](/wiki/Transition_metals "Transition metals"), including [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), [vanadium](/wiki/Vanadium "Vanadium"), and [chromium](/wiki/Chromium "Chromium"), tellurides,[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47)[[48]](#cite_note-Lasek2020-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Nakano2017-49) resulting in extremely clean samples with a thickness of only 0.5 monolayer.[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47)[[49]](#cite_note-Nakano2017-49)

The growth takes place in  [ultra-high vacuum (UHV)](/wiki/Ultra-high_vacuum "Ultra-high vacuum"). Precursors for the target materials are placed into evaporation cells, usually as powder (for example [selenium](/wiki/Selenium "Selenium")), or as a rod (for example [molybdenum](/wiki/Molybdenum "Molybdenum")).[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47) Some elements, such as selenium and [tellurium](/wiki/Tellurium "Tellurium"), both of which are [chalcogens](/wiki/Chalcogen "Chalcogen"), can be used in pure solid form as precursors. Some elements, however, can only be used when extracted from solid compounds, such as sulfur from FeS2. The compound materials are broken down by heating up the material at UHV pressures.[[50]](#cite_note-Hall2018-50) The evaporation cells are either [Knudsen cells](/wiki/Knudsen_cell "Knudsen cell") or [electron beam evaporation](/wiki/Electron-beam_physical_vapor_deposition "Electron-beam physical vapor deposition") based, depending on the materials; electron beam evaporation works with rods and can be used to reach high temperatures without overheating heating filaments, while Knudsen cells are suitable for powders and materials with a lower evaporation point. The evaporated materials are then directed towards the substrate; some common ones are [MoS2](/wiki/Molybdenum_disulfide "Molybdenum disulfide"), [HOPG](/wiki/Highly_oriented_pyrolytic_graphite "Highly oriented pyrolytic graphite"), [mica](/wiki/Mica "Mica"), or a sapphire substrate, such as [Al2O3](/wiki/Aluminium_oxide "Aluminium oxide").[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47)[[48]](#cite_note-Lasek2020-48)[[49]](#cite_note-Nakano2017-49)[[51]](#cite_note-Choudry2020-51) A specific substrate is chosen to fit the targeted growth the best. The substrate is kept heated during the process to enhance the growth, with the temperatures ranging from 300 °C to 700 °C. The temperature of the substrate is one key factor of the growth, and altering it can be used to grow different phases, such as 1T and 2H, of the same material.[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47)

MBE holds some advantages in regards to both manual exfoliation and CVD. Use of  [reflection high-energy electron diffraction (RHEED)](/wiki/Reflection_high-energy_electron_diffraction "Reflection high-energy electron diffraction") enables the in-situ monitoring of the growth, and this additionally with UHV and slow growth speed allows one to create clean, atomically thin monolayers.[[47]](#cite_note-Wei2020-47)[[52]](#cite_note-Singh2022-52) The improvement in sample quality is considerable when compared to exfoliation, as MBE is more effective in getting rid of the large flakes and impurities. In contrast to CVD, MBE proves beneficial when single-layerd TMDs are required.[[49]](#cite_note-Nakano2017-49)[[52]](#cite_note-Singh2022-52) The disadvantage of MBE is that it is a relatively complicated process that requires large amounts of specialized equipment. Maintaining UHV can be difficult, and the preparation of samples is slower than in the other two methods.

### Electrochemical Deposition

Electrodeposition is among the techniques that have emerged to produce TMDC semiconductors such as MoS2, WS2 and WSe2. Several reports have shown controlled electrodeposition of TMDC layers down to a monolayer.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)[[56]](#cite_note-56) The materials have so far shown continuous films of good uniformity but typically require annealing temperatures > 500 °C. Electrodepositions of TMDC films have been successfully reported over conducting films such as graphene and TiN, and over a SiO2 insulator by growing the TMDC laterally starting from a conductive film.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

### Colloidal Synthesis

A strategy for colloidal synthesis from soluble transistion metal and sulfur precursors was found recently, yielding highly defined nanoplatelets and nanosheets with a thickness of 1-2 monolayers.[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Electronic band structure

### Band gap

In the bulk form, TMD have an [indirect gap](/wiki/Direct_and_indirect_band_gaps "Direct and indirect band gaps") in the center of the [Brillouin zone](/wiki/Brillouin_zone "Brillouin zone"), whereas in [monolayer](/wiki/Monolayer "Monolayer") form the gap becomes [direct](/wiki/Direct_and_indirect_band_gaps "Direct and indirect band gaps") and is located in the K points.[[60]](#cite_note-Mak-60)[[2]](#cite_note-Splendiani-2)

### Spin–orbit coupling

Theoretical energies of the spin–orbit coupling[[61]](#cite_note-Cheng-61)[[62]](#cite_note-LiuShan-62)

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
|  | Valence band splitting (eV) | Conduction band splitting (eV) |
| MoS2 | 0.148 | 0.003 |
| WS2 | 0.430 | 0.026 |
| MoSe2 | 0.184 | 0.007 |
| WSe2 | 0.466 | 0.038 |
| MoTe2 | 0.219 | 0.034 |

splitting (eV)

splitting (eV)

For TMDs, the atoms are heavy and the outer layers electronic states are from d-orbitals that have a strong [spin–orbit coupling](/wiki/Spin%E2%80%93orbit_interaction "Spin–orbit interaction"). This spin orbit coupling removes the spins [degeneracy](/wiki/Degenerate_energy_levels "Degenerate energy levels") in both the conduction and valence band i.e. introduces a strong energy splitting between spin up and down states. In the case of MoS2, the spin splitting in conduction band is in the meV range, it is expected to be more pronounced in other material like WS2.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65) The spin orbit splitting in the valence band is several hundred meV.

### Spin-valley coupling and the electron valley degree of freedom

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/K_valley_TMDC_monolayer.jpg/500px-K_valley_TMDC_monolayer.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Monolayer_MoS2_photoluminesence_at_4K.png/500px-Monolayer_MoS2_photoluminesence_at_4K.png)

By controlling the charge or spin degree of freedom of carriers, as proposed by [spintronics](/wiki/Spintronics "Spintronics"), novel devices have already been made. If there are different conduction/valence band extrema in the electronic band structure in k-space, the carrier can be confined in one of these valleys. This degree of freedom opens up a new field of physics: the controlling of carriers k-valley index, also called [valleytronics](/wiki/Valleytronics "Valleytronics").[[22]](#cite_note-Xiao-22)[[66]](#cite_note-66)

For TMD monolayers crystals, the [parity symmetry](/wiki/Parity_(physics) "Parity (physics)") is broken, there is no more inversion center. K valleys of different directions in the 2D hexagonal Brillouin zone are no longer equivalent. So there are two kinds of K valley called K+ and K−. Also there is a strong energy degeneracy of different spin states in valence band. The transformation of one valley to another is described by the [time reversal operator](/wiki/T-symmetry "T-symmetry"). Moreover, crystal symmetry leads to valley dependent optical selection rules: a [right circular polarized photon](/wiki/Photon_polarization "Photon polarization") (σ+) initializes a carrier in the K+ valley and a left circular polarized photon (σ-) initializes a carrier in the K− valley.[[7]](#cite_note-ValleyCao-7) Thanks to these two properties (spin-valley coupling and optical selection rules), a laser of specific polarization and energy allows to initialize the electron valley states (K+ or K−) and spin states (up or down).[[1]](#cite_note-Eftekhari-1)

### Emission and absorption of light: excitons

A single layer of TMD can absorb up to 20% of incident light,[[5]](#cite_note-photodetector-5) which is unprecedented for such a thin material. When a photon of suitable energy is absorbed by a TMD monolayer, an electron is created in the conduction band; the electron now missing in the valence band is assimilated by a positively charged quasi-particle called a hole. The negatively charged electron and the positively charged hole are attracted via the [Coulomb interaction](/wiki/Coulomb%27s_law "Coulomb's law"), forming a bound state called an [exciton](/wiki/Exciton "Exciton") which can be thought as a [hydrogen atom](/wiki/Bohr_model "Bohr model") (with some difference). This Bosonic-like quasi-particle is very well known and studied in traditional semiconductors, such as [GaAs](/wiki/Gallium_arsenide "Gallium arsenide") and [ZnO](/wiki/Zinc_oxide "Zinc oxide") but in TMD it provides exciting new opportunities for applications and for studying fundamental physics. Indeed, the reduced [dielectric screening](/wiki/Electric-field_screening "Electric-field screening") and the [quantum size effect](/wiki/Potential_well#Quantum_confinement "Potential well") present in these ultrathin materials make the binding energy of excitons much stronger than those in traditional semiconductors. Binding energies of several hundreds of meV are observed for all the four principal members of the TMD family.[[21]](#cite_note-WangSHG-21)[[27]](#cite_note-Berkeley-27)[[67]](#cite_note-Chernikov-67)[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Excitons_as_hydrogenoid_energy_level.png/250px-Excitons_as_hydrogenoid_energy_level.png)

As mentioned before, we can think about an exciton as if it were a hydrogen atom, with an electron bound to a hole. The main difference is that this system is not stable and tends to relax to the vacuum state, which is here represented by an electron in the valence band. The energy difference between the exciton 'ground state' (n=1) and the 'vacuum state' is called optical gap and is the energy of the photon emitted when an exciton recombines. This is the energy of the photons emitted by TMD monolayers and observed as huge emission peaks in photoluminescence (PL) experiments, such as the one labelled X0 in the figure. In this picture the binding energy EB is defined as the difference between the free particle band gap and the optical band gap and represent, as usual, the energy needed to take the hole and the electron apart. The existence of this energy difference is called band gap renormalization. The analogy with hydrogen atom doesn't stop here as excitonic excited states were observed at higher energies and with different techniques.[[21]](#cite_note-WangSHG-21)[[67]](#cite_note-Chernikov-67)

Because of the spin–orbit splitting of the valence band two different series of excitons exist in TMD, called A- and B-excitons. In the A series the hole is located in the upper branch of the Valence band while for the B-exciton the hole is in the lower branch. As a consequence the optical gap for B-exciton is larger and the corresponding peak is found at higher energy in PL and reflectivity measurements.

Another peak usually appears in the PL spectra of TMD monolayers, which is associated to different quasi-particles called [trions](/wiki/Trion_(physics) "Trion (physics)").[[71]](#cite_note-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72) These are excitons bound to another free carrier which can be either an electron or a hole. As a consequence a trion is a negative or positively charged complex. The presence of a strong trion peak in a PL spectrum, eventually stronger than the peak associated with exciton recombination, is a signature of a doped monolayer. It is believed now that this doping is extrinsic, which means that it arises from charged trap states present in the substrate (generally SiO2). Positioning a TMD monolayer between two flakes of hBN removes this extrinsic doping and greatly increase the optical quality of the sample.[[70]](#cite_note-Upconv-70)[[73]](#cite_note-Fablinew-73)

At higher excitation powers [biexcitons](/wiki/Biexciton "Biexciton")[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) have also been observed in monolayer TMDs. These complexes are formed by two bound excitons. Theory predicts that even larger charge-carrier complexes, such as charged biexcitons (quintons) and ion-bound biexcitons, are stable and should be visible in the PL spectra.[[76]](#cite_note-76) Additionally, quantum light has been observed to originate from point defects in these materials in a variety of configurations.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78)[[79]](#cite_note-79)[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)[[82]](#cite_note-82)

## Radiation effects of TMD monolayers

Common forms of radiation used to create defects in TMDs are particle and electromagnetic irradiation, impactin

enriched_points.expanded_content: TMDs are atomically thin semiconductors composed of transition metals and chalcogens, exhibiting unique properties such as direct bandgaps and strong spin–orbit coupling, enabling diverse electronic applications.
hBN is a 2D material known for its insulating properties and thermal stability. It serves as an ideal substrate for TMDs, enhancing their performance in electronic and optoelectronic devices.

MXenes are conductive 2D materials derived from transition metal carbides and nitrides, showcasing high electrical conductivity and hydrophilicity, which enable applications in energy storage and water purification.
TMOs are a broad class of materials that display a range of electronic and magnetic properties, making them suitable for applications such as sensors, catalysts, and memory devices.

Unlike graphene's zero bandgap, TMDs possess tunable bandgaps, offering advantages in semiconductor applications. This distinction enhances their potential for use in transistors and optoelectronic devices.
